snippet: Today's guest expert is ML pro Arthur Mello. He's here to share a portfolio-ready tutorial to help you build applied AI skills. View in browser Educative Денис, Today I'm excited to welcome
subject: Tutorial: Use AI to build a startup pitch deck
body:
Today's guest expert is ML pro Arthur Mello. He's here to share a portfolio-ready tutorial to help you build applied AI skills.

View in browser (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWnfzx2ntj87N8KJdzN5HN9jW7gFL9V5Lk9SZN8NyyFq5m_5PW7Y9pgv6lZ3mDW1mHb-q792h4bW3DwZXf5jShvvW8qsbg63pyHR3W4hxM2N2GNYZZW3hbr6Y5xK33WW8T6cRY2V76WTW72BfX986fhSGW3ks_Sf5QtS-BV3KTS79fF-84W5lvwTZ5qqm0yW8w8tGp9bBqpgW2-xrQD5r22m9W8WKHtz2Bt5MFW70c2Qy8kl3qMW6sZNvK5f9XY6N6fd-lXlvrMVW1y4twS7VcxnkW6P7GKn70Byg5VP4P7_3_dL5kW3W99MQ44VQ1TW42Cd9p9d3FP6VFSCWd3hx1y7W48G6fg1TX032W30yfCd5CGMxJW1zFJG04V0Jv5N5B1xrB95wTsW57gWmx50Vl0ZVCXg4X3mfJbDVWKFg61wqVC3N2XZ6yH-DqzqW1Tcdbr47B1TdN7_YLpWLnS_7W2-Xj-J4B4GZbW8bws4W31DvffW2777_-35LMx1W6TthYV1fypGbW1P0QH18n0XzfW6Gw8yg1rGxX7W94nbkR8hvH9MVYRTw16qwKSpW2dqBJN1xcy_-W6yxycY70HVhJf8jt8d004 )

Educative (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWnfzx2ntj87N8KJdzN5HN9jW7gFL9V5Lk9SZN8NyyFK3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3lrW3CyL4K101ctHW5k7pXs2rBf86W6LJcGZ8MdYt4W8Hb4548Z6QngW2ZYRTn54PzwDW6_-ScD3ngJN5W8R3Y-V5vnnDLW6f5th31njrmwVf_ZBR97ldtNW5SS63R45RJp8W5kTTpT5WdbBkW772XQB6tVchCW5mvTRr1f02MkN8kWtKWwc00lW7Ktwgw48gkCvW5gpF_14wLBbgW1VvrhB71pbZ2W5y_CfD6-JN1nVvG5Gj5gGNZVW7B7Dz65Ll6sbW711W7y87M7MXW6_4gtd385JyDN9ctR4KJ6B9SV5PXjn80fTkRW1pP8mw4vwC1nW5bmlhg36cspNW5R3Q6M3CsJz8W7VR1YS3377WMdgL5Mn04 )

Денис,

Today I'm excited to welcome today's guest expert: machine learning pro Arthur Mello.

Arthur isn’t here to talk theory. He’s sharing a hands-on, portfolio-ready build that shows how to go beyond toy LLM demos and ship something that actually feels like a product in 2026.

If you're ready to go from an AI user to an AI builder (without any setup or downloads), you can access hundreds of AI courses and projects on Educative with our Premium Plus subscription. It's available to you today at 50% off (or more) (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWnfzx2ntj87N8KJdzN5HN9jW7gFL9V5Lk9SZN8NyyFK3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3lrW3CyL4K101ctHW5k7pXs2rBf86W6LJcGZ8MdYt4W8Hb4548Z6QngW2ZYRTn54PzwDW6_-ScD3ngJN5W8R3Y-V5vnnDLW6f5th31njrmwVf_ZBR97ldtNW5SS63R45RJp8W5kTTpT5WdbBkW772XQB6tVchCW5mvTRr1f02MkN8kWtKWwc00lW7Ktwgw48gkCvW5gpF_14wLBbgW1VvrhB71pbZ2W5y_CfD6-JN1nVvG5Gj5gGNZVW7B7Dz65Ll6sbW711W7y87M7MXW6_4gtd385JyDN9ctR4KJ6B9SV5PXjn80fTkRW1pP8mw4vwC1nW5bmlhg36cspNW5R3Q6M3CsJz8W7VR1YS3377WMdgL5Mn04 ) .

Now over to Arthur!

Hola everyone, Arthur here 🫡

Today, I’m sharing a hands-on build guide that’s perfect if you’ve been experimenting with LLMs and want to ship something that feels like a real product - not just another chatbot demo.

The article walks you through creating a multi-agent AI system that generates a complete startup pitch deck from a single idea. You give it a one-line concept (e.g., “a platform for neighbourhood equipment sharing”), and it outputs the full package: market research, product definition, financial projections, and a pitch deck outline - all wired into a demo-ready UI.

What makes this especially useful is that it focuses on the engineering patterns behind agent systems: clear roles, tool integration, orchestration, and structured outputs. These are the same building blocks you’ll reuse in more serious AI applications.

Architecture at a glance

The system uses a simple, disciplined workflow driven by a central orchestrator. Instead of having agents debate endlessly, the orchestrator runs a linear pipeline: it receives the startup idea, triggers each agent in sequence, and passes only the necessary context forward. That’s what keeps outputs predictable and the build weekend-friendly.

The pipeline looks like this:

Setting up the environment

The guide emphasizes getting a stable project foundation so you don’t end up with a messy pile of scripts once you add more agents.

It recommends using uv (fast installs + isolated environment) and installing a small set of core dependencies:

-

smolagents

-

OpenAI SDK

-

Streamlit

-

python-dotenv

-

pydantic

You store secrets in a .env file (e.g., OPENAI_API_KEY=...) and load them via python-dotenv.

The repo layout should stay modular from day one. Here’s the recommended structure:

Choosing an agent framework

The guide also gives a practical overview of the agent framework ecosystem:

-

LangChain: feature-heavy and flexible, but can feel “too much” for a weekend build.

-

LlamaIndex: strong for retrieval/document pipelines and structured flows.

-

CrewAI: geared toward multi-agent teamwork and role/task abstractions.

For this project, it uses HuggingFace’s smolagents because it’s lightweight, transparent, and keeps tool definitions simple (often just a Python function). That transparency is valuable while learning how orchestration works - you can see what the agent is doing and why, without wrestling with a large framework.

The key point: once you understand the prompt → tool call → output cycle in one framework, switching later is straightforward because the underlying principles stay the same.

What you’ll build: “Startup-in-a-Box”

Think of it like a compact startup team - four specialised agents working in sequence:

1) Building the Market Analyst agent

This agent acts like a skeptical researcher. Instead of guessing numbers, it’s designed to use search/tooling to validate assumptions and bring back external signals.

It produces a structured report with:

-

Problem definition

-

Target users + demand signals

-

TAM/SAM/SOM estimates (rough but grounded)

-

Competitors and positioning

-

Risks, gaps, and uncertainties

-

Suggested pricing and business model

A key point the guide emphasizes: if the idea is vague (no clear user/problem), the agent should ask one clarifying question, then proceed. That keeps the system helpful without turning into an endless Q&A loop.

2) Building the Product Manager agent

Next, the Product Manager takes the market report and converts it into a focused product spec. Importantly, it’s instructed not to rewrite the market analysis. Instead, it extracts only what matters and produces an actionable definition.

Output includes:

-

Product overview (what it is + who it serves)

-

Core value proposition

-

Key features (3–6 essentials)

-

Constraints or considerations based on market realities

This stage is where the “startup idea” stops being a vague concept and starts looking like something you could actually build.

3) Building the Financial Analyst agent

This is the stage that makes the deck feel more complete, and it’s also where format discipline matters most.

The Financial Analyst takes the market + product inputs and generates a coherent financial narrative with a 5-year projection. The guide recommends enforcing a strict JSON schema so downstream steps don’t break.

JSON output includes:

-

Assumptions (pricing model, conversion/adoption, key costs, notes)

-

Year-by-year revenue, costs, profit (Years 1–5)

-

Required upfront investment

It’s not about perfect finance modelling. It’s about producing numbers that are consistent with earlier assumptions and packaged in a machine-readable format (great if you later export to a spreadsheet).

4) Building the Pitch agent

Finally, the Pitch Creator takes all outputs and turns them into a concise, investor-style pitch deck outline.

The guide strongly recommends generating the deck in Marp Markdown (instead of PPTX) because:

-

Markdown is more forgiving

-

It’s stable for LLM generation

-

It’s fast to preview and export

To avoid inconsistent decks, it uses a fixed slide structure, such as:

-

Title

-

Problem

-

Market Insights

-

Product

-

Features

-

Financials

-

Why Now

-

Closing

This prevents the model from inventing random slide formats each run and keeps the output predictable.

Implementing the orchestrator workflow

The standout design choice here is the linear orchestrator:

Market → Product → Finance → Pitch

No debates, no “agents arguing,” no messy shared memory. It’s sequential, transparent, and easy to debug — which is exactly what you want when you’re building something end-to-end.

The orchestrator’s job is simple:

-

Trigger agents in order

-

Pass only the necessary context between them

-

Validate outputs (especially JSON)

-

Retry or correct malformed responses before continuing

This is a very practical blueprint for anyone trying to build reliable agent pipelines.

A clean project structure

Another thing I liked: it recommends a modular repo layout from day one:

-

agents/ (each agent isolated and tweakable)

-

tools/ (reusable helper functions, search utilities, finance helpers)

-

orchestrator/ (workflow logic separate from UI)

-

frontend/ (Streamlit app)

-

tests/ (unit tests + agent tests as outputs get more structured)

This is the kind of structure that makes your demo feel “production-minded,” even if it’s a weekend project.

Adding evaluation with a Judge LLM

One of the most practical extensions is adding a small “Judge” agent to score each stage - not as ground truth, but as a fast feedback loop.

The judge scores each output (1–5) on:

-

clarity

-

structure

-

relevance

-

internal logic

And returns JSON with short notes. This mirrors how real teams iterate on prompts and outputs: you generate → evaluate → refine prompts and schemas.

Integrating everything into a Streamlit interface

The final step is integrating the pipeline into a Streamlit interface so anyone can try it:

-

user enters an idea

-

the system runs the pipeline

-

outputs appear in sections (Market / Product / Finance / Deck)

-

deck is downloadable as Markdown

If you want to make it even more impressive, the guide suggests future add-ons like:

-

image generation for UI mockups

-

a Growth Strategist agent

-

tech feasibility reviewer

-

PPTX export using python-pptx (more control, more brittle)

Quality checks

Once the pipeline is working, the guide suggests running a few intentionally minimal checks. These don’t measure polish - they measure correctness and help you pinpoint where something went wrong.

-

Market Analyst: uses search evidence, follows the same structure every run, and asks for missing context when needed

-

Product Manager: stays concise, features clearly map to market insights, output fields remain consistent

-

Financial Analyst: returns schema-valid JSON, includes 5-year revenue/cost/profit, upfront investment looks reasonable

-

Pitch Creator: follows the slide outline, tells a clear story, and avoids long copy-paste from earlier stages

-

Orchestrator + Streamlit: runs without manual fixes, renders cleanly, and deck downloads without errors.

What I like about this project isn’t the pitch deck itself - it’s that it demonstrates real AI engineering skills: tool-augmented agents, clean orchestration, structured outputs, validation, and a demo UI. If you want a portfolio project that looks like a real system, this is a strong template.

If you’d like to learn more about how to build AI agents and agentic systems, Educative's course: Build AI Agents and Multi-Agent Systems with CrewAI (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWnfzx2ntj87N8KJdzN5HN9jW7gFL9V5Lk9SZN8NyyDx5m_5PW69t95C6lZ3mLW13xKZ371Kw3JW4HV7kB4vd5DtW49ZY1b1DKqXDW2cCwjt6BShZFW40m7-V26Kn4CVCFcPl3QcKPYW4NqdfW1ysxG8W8MHTDv1wFH-2Vg_PXn2DGRPJW4PrSZP8cTd16W72wk-L4w8qTSW6mLvfJ834Nx4W8RZC8g7FCQSVW3d1-k02mCRcKW1v0Jts73MJjvW8dCWC811RXlcW6Lw79_2M-5hQW68WfhL6mXhs4W4_DQTK3xDfz9N5ZZ5yvr1vbdW4tKL521jNvSJW2FfDVQ2F4CWGW1GTzSW48kcyyW69K06D39JpxxN89DDhXsJWkGW2Tlj5f1bDF5mW11q8Cs6RRntwN25Bn7TT1Yl0W6M94WP7Cdq46VlNsS23_g5LjW3XwW9J6kwcHMW5TKjkM3-N-xHW1GVn4R2R4rvrW30D2d_3fl8G5W7Mksk2715v6zW328PR61H8b86f76Hm5K04 ) will guide you from the basics to implementation with the multi-agent platform CrewAI.

I hope this was helpful to you.

Arthur Mello

Data scientist and educator
